speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was taken directly to Auschwitz from Ungvar. He was living in Ungvar at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was living in Ungvar till deportation time, OK? Whenever that happened. Later on, I found out that it happened fairly quickly. And he was deported fairly quickly to Auschwitz.  To Auschwitz. So it was very quick, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you asked me a few questions. Would you mind if I answer them not in sequence, OK but-- OK, now [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] life at this point in Budapest was somewhat confusing, obviously, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] somewhat confusing. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Can we stop for a minute? No, OK, it was somewhat confusing. What was confusing about it?  [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What did you think was going to be happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what's going to be happening. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We didn't know what was going to be happening. And--  [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things ever change? Were there-- if somebody got sick, were there doctors available for them that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember having any doctors. Maybe we all be very healthy. But I don't remember having any doctors, or needing any doctors. OK, now this life-- and I call it life-- was continuing more or less in the same beat [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] till about the 24th of-- the 24th of-- [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] the 24th of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. I'm getting a little confused now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was it in the winter, in the spring, in the summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, it was winter. It was winter. And it was definitely winter. And-- [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] [DRINKS] I'm sorry. What's your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Adele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a change that happened to that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The change-- the change was that-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] that-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] there was a change in the ghetto that-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] you could feel that things were speeding up. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] things were speeding up. We knew that there was a-- things are speeding up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no, yes and no. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was like things were speeding up. Yet, we don't know whether it was for the good or for the bad. You know, so it's like-- it's hard to explain. It's hard to explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did that change? Did it ever slow down? Did it ever speed up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no it didn't slow down. It didn't speed up. It became-- it became-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] it became-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] it became-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] how should I put it? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, it-- it became-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you felt like during all this time. What did you-- what did you think? Because you were a young boy at the time. What did you think was happening? What did your cousin think? You were both together. Were you with other children also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my cousin was younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was-- don't forget. We are so young. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were-- we were children. So I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to other children about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no other children. There really-- there were no other children to talk to. And-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me a typical day that you had in the ghetto. From the time you got up, what was it like? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical day, there were no typical days. There really were no typical days, because you don't know what a day would bring. There were no typical days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give an example of any day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Give an example of a day in the ghetto, any day.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I can't give you an example. There were some days when-- [PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS] there's no typical days in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stands in your mind during this time in the ghetto at all? If there are anything that you want to tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I can't think of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you liberated in this? Were you liberated while you were in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was liberated on the-- on the 18th of-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] of 18th of-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] of 18th of 19-- oh, shit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They liberated me. And they tore off the yellow star. And it was very-- very moving. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it was a very moving experience. They tore off the Russian-- they tore off the yellow star. And they said, you'll never have to wear it again, and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. It was-- and-- and, that's how we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after liberation? Did you stay in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, friend of-- friend of my uncle's [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] saw the-- had a friend-- had a friend in-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Romania who had friends there and said that he knows things are OK in-- in Romania, and things are OK. So-- and-- things are-- in Romania, things are already more or less like-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] things are like-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] like more like war, or more like war time-- more like peace time. And things are OK. And-- and that's how--

speaker: interviewer
text: So is that where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go alone? Or did you go with your-- with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my family, and some other people. And we-- we went there-- we went there-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage there? Was it all right for you in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful, just like peacetime. You know, it was like peacetime. And we-- we went there. And we stayed there for six-- for seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Budapest. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay in Budapest after that? Or did you go to another country?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went-- stayed in Budapest. We went back to Budapest. We went to Buda-- we went to Budapest. And we went back to school. And things were great. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the non-Jews treat you at that point? Was there a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did the non-Jews treat us at that point--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, no, no, I can't say they were any different, you know. They were different [INAUDIBLE], right. They were different [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Again, I was too young, you know? I was too young to differentiate, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you went back to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I graduated from high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did-- what happened after that? What did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, I was waiting to-- to get-- I was waiting to get-- I was waiting to get finished with high school, and finish high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in any DP camps, or have any dealings with people in DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.  No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to any other country except for Romania after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And then what happened? You finished high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished high school. I went to-- I went to Romania. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which country did you go to after you were in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bless you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was in Hungary, I went to Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Romania. Then you came back and you finished high school in Budapest, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that correct? And then after you finished high school in Budapest, how long did you stay in Budapest after that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long did I stay in Budapest after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: After you graduated high school, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand your question. After I graduated high school--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much longer did you remain there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For four-- four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to-- then we went to-- I see. Then we went to high school-- not high school-- then went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from Budapest straight to the United States, or did you go to another country first?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I personally came to Budapest through Saturday-- through Saturday. I personally went to Budapest through-- through-- [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] getting confused now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- I went to Romania-- not Romania. I went to-- I went to-- I'm getting very confused now. So what do you want to do? We'll continue when you came to the United States. Do you remember when you came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when I came to the United States, I came to the United States, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you like the United States when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Loved it, loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people who were here ask you about your war experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I had family here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- what did they say when you told them about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can you say it like? Unbelievable, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay as religious once you get here as you were before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay as religious once you got here as you were before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.  No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you do you feel that your experience during the war affected how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Less religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do while you were here? What kind of business did you go into? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do? I went-- I went into sales. And I went into sales. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get married here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away, but eventually I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name? Her name is Charlotte.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have-- we have three children. We have-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] four grandchildren. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to do this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did I want to? Because I think it's a-- it's a must. It's an absolute must. It should be done. It has to be done. It's-- it's-- it's-- I can't even put it in a stronger way, than just-- it just-- there's no reason why this should not be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else, or any other thing that you would like to say about what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said-- I said it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message for your children, your grandchildren, and for future generations about what you went through? Is there a message you'd like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: See to it that it's never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I'm Charlotte Rosenhaus-Krauss. And I am Imi's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say about your husband, Imi James?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking at everything from all views, I think we've had a pretty good life together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you met your husband, did he ever talk to you about his war experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel as a non-survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt that there but for the grace of God went I. And I was very interested in all that happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, I'm Robert Krauss. I'm Imi's son, oldest son, oldest child, 30-- 38 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, could you describe how it felt to have-- be a child of a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Sorry. Proud, in a way. I-- I thought it-- it made us kind of special. Oh boy, hold on-- well, I was totally fixated on, how could this happen to me-- to me, to him, to all of us. I couldn't understand. As a little boy, I couldn't understand how the atrocities that my father told me about. And-- and he was wonderful. And I'm very grate-- grateful for him being the kind of survivor that was-- that was open about his experience. And he-- he spoke-- probably spoke a lot more than he just spoke to you. But he spoke, and gave a lot of details. And it-- it just created an awareness in me that I can't believe people could do this to one another. I had total, total daydreams about what I would do to Hitler as a kid. I would fantasize about what would be the best way to kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I never looked at it as a burden. I-- I enjoyed the knowledge. We used to watch all the documentaries together on-- on PBS. He introduced me to Elie Weisel, all the writings. And it was, you know, a fascinating compelling-- compelling part of my life. I can go on as long as I want? Thank you. What else? As long as you give your sister a chance to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, she'll have a chance. I guess that's it. You're going to come back to me? I can't speak to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think it affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The way I raised my children? Well, I-- it's great. I have a nine-year-old. I have three children, a nine-year-old Rachel, a six-year-old David, and soon to be three-year-old Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Kim, thank you. And Rachel is also fixated on the topic. She, you know, just took out the book from school. And she also, you know, strange as it may or may be, you know-- it's no coincidence. She doesn't understand why Adolf Hitler did what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's real interesting seeing her go through the same stuff that I went through, like how incredulous it is. Why did they do this to-- to grandpa's family? You know, it just-- it's so-- it makes no sense from adults-- from an adult's perspective. It totally makes no sense from a child's perspective. But I think it's so wonderful that-- that she already has that sense of-- of, you know, indignity about what took place, because I personally think that it is so, so critical for people to-- to feel as much as they can feel the pain of what-- of what the victims went through, not only survivors, but actual victims, people who died in the most horrible way, to see, you know, children separated from parents. How intense of a hurt was that? And I guess I'm really indebted to anybody who-- who takes time out of their lives, you know, whatever generation is listening to this, to say, wait a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life might be going great. But I got to feel that pain as an honor, as an honor, to the people that-- that perished. You should feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now.
